evaluation.manager_summary: Dennis, a prior residential patient with 14 months sobriety, relapsed 4 months ago and is drinking daily (bottle to bottle-and-a-half of wine). He requested residential treatment again, reporting mild withdrawal symptoms. Kendra captured all clinical screening elements, validated his relapse context, secured insurance details, and set clear next steps with benefits verification and callback scheduled for 1-2 PM today. Excellent rapport-building and compliance discipline throughout.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent immediately acknowledged Dennis's sobriety achievement and reframed his return call as a strength rather than a failure. The tone was warm, validating, and deeply person-centered from the first response, well within 60 seconds.

Agent captured carrier, member ID, and group number correctly and framed verification as a step toward help. The one missed element was not explicitly stating 'pending verification' when describing the next steps, though the process itself was handled well.

Agent systematically captured substance type, frequency, duration, withdrawal symptoms, and prior treatment history. Stayed clearly in the intake role, asking about symptoms without diagnosing or characterizing them clinically.

Agent correctly assessed this as a this-week need rather than a crisis. Dennis presented with moderate withdrawal symptoms and relapse but no acute safety signals. Agent scheduled appropriately for same-day callback without manufacturing urgency.
Caller is the patient himself, confirmed throughout the transcript.
Dennis was fully cooperative throughout with no expressed concerns, hesitations, or resistance. He was clear about needing help and willing to proceed with all steps.

Agent provided specific timing (benefits verification within 90 minutes, callback between 1-2 PM), clear sequencing (verification then admissions coordinator call), and confirmed caller availability. Ownership and next actions were crystal clear.

Agent maintained excellent boundaries overall, deferring to clinical team for plan design and formal assessment. One borderline moment: 'your body remembers' could be read as light clinical characterization, but it was immediately followed by proper deferral to the clinical team. No diagnostic labels, outcome promises, or scope overreach.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Dennis, thank you for being so upfront about your history. Fourteen months of sobriety after residential — that's real work.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: And the fact that you're calling now, rather than waiting for things to get worse, tells me you haven't forgotten what it felt like to be on the other side of this.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: They're not excuses — they're context, and our clinical team needs that context to design the right plan for you.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Let me get your insurance information so we can check what your plan covers for a residential admission.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Sure. It's Meridian Care, member ID BP93660796, group GRP-444091.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Good. That continuity actually helps. I'm noting your prior treatment facility, your length of sobriety, and the relapse timeline.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Can I ask: how much are you drinking now versus before you got into treatment the first time?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: And are you having any withdrawal symptoms when you try to go a day without?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Given that you've been through residential, what's your thinking on level of care this time?

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Some. Anxiety, trouble sleeping. Nothing as bad as last time, but it's there.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Good to know. That history matters — your body remembers. I want to flag that for our clinical team.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: benefits verification will be back to you within ninety minutes, and our admissions coordinator will call immediately after with availability for an assessment.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: benefits verification will be back to you within ninety minutes, and our admissions coordinator will call immediately after with availability for an assessment.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Can you take a call this afternoon?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Perfect — you'll hear from us between one and two o'clock.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Good to know. That history matters — your body remembers. I want to flag that for our clinical team.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: They're not excuses — they're context, and our clinical team needs that context to design the right plan for you.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Our clinical team will assess that formally, but your own read matters.
